title: How to publish a GEO resource page with auto-geo
metaDescription: A walkthrough of publishing a GEO resource page via the auto-geo /api/resources/publish endpoint, including the payload shape and validation behavior.
excerpt: A focused guide to calling the auto-geo publish endpoint: payload shape, auth, validation errors, and the soft-warning iteration loop.

author.name: Jane Doe
author.bio: Jane writes about generative engine optimization and the architecture of pages that AI search engines cite. Previously: search at a large platform.

targetQueries: how to publish a GEO resource page
geoMetadata.primaryFunction: Walk a developer through publishing a single GEO resource page with auto-geo end to end.
geoMetadata.informationGainStatement: First-party walkthrough of the auto-geo publish flow including the warnings iteration pattern, which is not documented elsewhere.

tldr.text: Publishing with auto-geo means issuing an authenticated POST to /api/resources/publish with a JSON payload that satisfies the seven-block GEO architecture; the endpoint validates against a strict Zod schema, persists via your configured storage adapter, and returns the page URL plus any soft quality warnings for optional iteration.

blocks.text: This guide covers the auto-geo publish flow end to end: the payload shape, the auth header, the validation behavior, and what to do with the warnings array. By the end, you should be able to publish a resource page from curl, from a Node script, or from an AI agent via the included MCP server.

sections.heading: What does the publish payload look like?
sections.answerCapsule: The payload is a JSON object with identity fields (slug, title, metaDescription, category, excerpt), an author object, ISO publish and modified dates, geoMetadata, optional entity references, and the seven body blocks (tldr, intro, sections, relatedGuides, keyTakeaways, faq, disclosure). The full Zod schema in core/schema.ts is the source of truth for shape and constraints.

blocks.text: Every prose field accepts the same inline syntax: **bold**, *italic*, and [label](url). Raw HTML is rejected at the schema boundary. URLs starting with a slash render as internal links; everything else is external. The constraint is intentional — see the [schema source](https://github.com/shadowresearch/auto-geo/blob/main/core/schema.ts) for the rationale.

sections.heading: How does authentication work?
sections.answerCapsule: Set the GEO_PUBLISH_TOKEN environment variable on the server to a long random string and include it on every request as an Authorization: Bearer header. The token is read at request time, so rotating it takes effect immediately. There is intentionally no session or cookie auth — this is a server-to-server API.

blocks.text: Generate the token with openssl rand -hex 32 or your secret manager's equivalent. Treat it like a long-lived API key: store it in the deployment provider's secret manager, never commit it to source, and rotate it on a regular cadence. See the [Vercel docs on env vars](https://vercel.com/docs/projects/environment-variables) for the standard pattern.

items.title: What is a GEO resource page?

items: Publishing requires a Bearer token matching GEO_PUBLISH_TOKEN; the endpoint is server-to-server and intentionally has no session auth.
Hard validation errors come back as HTTP 400 with an issues array pointing at the offending field path.
Soft quality warnings come back as a warnings array on HTTP 200 success responses; addressing them is optional.
The publish endpoint is idempotent on slug — re-posting the same slug overwrites the existing entry.

items.question: What happens if validation fails?
items.answer: The endpoint returns HTTP 400 with a JSON body of the form { error, issues } where issues is an array of { path, message } objects. The path is a dotted reference to the field that failed; the message describes the constraint. Fix the payload and re-post to the same slug — the endpoint is idempotent.

items.question: How are reserved slugs handled?
items.answer: Reserved slugs are configured per deployment via the reservedSlugs option passed to createNextHandlers or createHonoRouter. Publishing to a reserved slug returns HTTP 409, protecting statically routed pages from being overwritten via the API. Configure this with your app's static page list.

items.question: Can I publish a resource without storage configured?
items.answer: No. The publish endpoint requires a working storage adapter and returns HTTP 502 if the adapter throws. For local development, use the in-memory adapter; for production, use KV or Supabase. The render path tolerates missing storage by 404-ing, but the publish path must succeed.

disclosure.text: This is a sample payload included with the auto-geo next-minimal example app. It satisfies all hard schema constraints but is deliberately short for demo purposes — it will surface several soft warnings on publish, which mirrors the real iteration loop.
